hypothesis: One installable JSON-first command registry over canonical modules can reach command-architecture parity while remaining smaller, safer, and less duplicative than a collection of independent wrappers.

strongest_objections: The command surface exposes eleven commands while Claude SEO documents twenty-five; breadth parity is not achieved and remains assigned to later capability rows.
Several specialist agents are explicitly advisory and cannot yet execute their own domain-specific command.
No new live data source is added, so this cycle improves usability and wiring rather than evidence acquisition.
residual_risks: Users could mistake command architecture parity for full command breadth parity.

strongest_objections: Editable installation is proven, but signed release artifacts and clean-machine distribution remain unproven until the release phase.
The command registry is a second metadata file and therefore must remain continuously cross-validated against the canonical capability registry.
The broad exception boundary in the CLI protects JSON output but future provider handlers must preserve sanitized, typed failure semantics.
residual_risks: Future command additions may expand the interface faster than documentation and ownership checks unless CI remains mandatory.

lessons: A small command registry can improve usability without duplicating the domain implementation.
Every command owner must be validated against the canonical capability registry.
Generated command documentation prevents user-facing CLI drift.
Command architecture parity must not be represented as execution-depth parity.
next_action: Submit the green branch through the normal human-controlled squash merge workflow, while keeping deeper command capability rows open.
